title: Heapify array
description: Build a min-heap using heapify operation

examples.explanation: Start with arr = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]. i=1 (element 1 at index 1): Compare with parent at index 0 (3): 1 < 3, swap -> [1, 3, 4, 1, 5]. j becomes 0, stop. i=2 (element 4 at index 2): Parent at index 0 (1): 4 >= 1, no swap. i=3 (element 1 at index 3): Parent at index 1 (3): 1 < 3, swap -> [1, 1, 4, 3, 5]. j becomes 1, parent at index 0 (1): 1 >= 1, no swap. i=4 (element 5 at index 4): Parent at index 1 (1): 5 >= 1, no swap. Final heap: [1, 1, 4, 3, 5].

approaches.name: Approach 1: Naive Insertion O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the array and insert each element into an initially empty heap by repeatedly bubbling up from the end. This builds the heap incrementally but is inefficient for bulk construction.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) in-place or O(n) if using extra space

ladders.title: Full Problem — Build Min-Heap via Naive Insertion
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of building a min-heap by iteratively inserting elements and bubbling up.
ladders.explanation: This approach builds a min-heap from an unsorted array by treating it as an initially empty heap and inserting elements one by one. For each insertion, we append the element to the end of the heap (which is the array) and then 'bubble up' or 'percolate up' from that position to restore the min-heap property. The min-heap property requires that every parent node be less than or equal to its children. The intuition is that by inserting elements sequentially and fixing violations upward, we gradually build a valid min-heap. This method is straightforward but inefficient for bulk construction because each insertion takes O(log n) time, leading to O(n log n) total time. It's in-place if we modify the input array directly, using O(1) extra space, or O(n) if we use a separate heap array. The algorithm starts with an empty heap (or the first element as the root), iterates through the array from the second element onward, and for each element, calls a helper function to bubble it up from its current index. The connection to higher levels is that this is the complete solution using the naive insertion approach. Common mistakes include incorrect parent-child index calculations (e.g., using 0-based indexing where parent = (i-1)//2), not handling the root case (index 0 has no parent), or forgetting to update the array in-place. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize heap as the input array (or copy it). 2. For i from 1 to n-1 (assuming heap[0] is the first element): a. Set current index = i. b. While current index > 0 and heap[current index] < heap[parent index]: i. Swap heap[current index] and heap[parent index]. ii. Update current index = parent index. 3. Return the heap array. Time complexity is O(n log n) because there are n-1 insertions, each taking up to O(log n) time for bubbling. Space complexity is O(1) in-place. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def heapify_naive(arr): n = len(arr); for i in range(1, n): j = i; while j > 0 and arr[j] < arr[(j-1)//2]: arr[j], arr[(j-1)//2] = arr[(j-1)//2], arr[j]; j = (j-1)//2; return arr.

examples.trace: Start with arr = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]. i=1 (element 1 at index 1): Compare with parent at index 0 (3): 1 < 3, swap -> [1, 3, 4, 1, 5]. j becomes 0, stop. i=2 (element 4 at index 2): Parent at index 0 (1): 4 >= 1, no swap. i=3 (element 1 at index 3): Parent at index 1 (3): 1 < 3, swap -> [1, 1, 4, 3, 5]. j becomes 1, parent at index 0 (1): 1 >= 1, no swap. i=4 (element 5 at index 4): Parent at index 1 (1): 5 >= 1, no swap. Final heap: [1, 1, 4, 3, 5].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Bubble Up Operation
ladders.desc: Implement the bubble-up function to restore min-heap property after insertion.
ladders.explanation: The bubble-up operation (also called percolate-up) is the core helper function in the naive insertion approach. It ensures that after inserting a new element at the end of the heap, the min-heap property is maintained by moving the element up the tree as needed. The intuition is that if a child node is smaller than its parent, it violates the min-heap property, so we swap them and continue checking upwards until the property holds or we reach the root. This works because swapping a smaller child with its parent makes the parent smaller, potentially fixing violations at that level, but may introduce new violations higher up, so we recurse upward. The algorithm takes the heap array and the index of the element to bubble up (typically the last inserted index). It uses a while loop: while the index is greater than 0 (not the root) and the element at that index is less than its parent, swap with the parent and update the index to the parent's index. The parent index in a 0-based array is (i-1)//2 for index i. Time complexity is O(log n) in the worst case, as the element may bubble up to the root, traveling the height of the tree. Space complexity is O(1) as it operates in-place. This sub-routine feeds into the full problem by being called for each insertion. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in parent calculation, infinite loops if the condition is incorrect, or not handling equal values properly (in a min-heap, parent <= child, so stop if equal). Real code pattern: def bubble_up(heap, idx): while idx > 0 and heap[idx] < heap[(idx-1)//2]: heap[idx], heap[(idx-1)//2] = heap[(idx-1)//2], heap[idx]; idx = (idx-1)//2.

examples.trace: Start: heap = [2, 3, 4, 5], idx=3. Parent index = (3-1)//2 = 1, parent value = 3. Compare 5 < 3? False, so no swap. Heap remains [2, 3, 4, 5].

Start: heap = [3, 4, 5, 1], idx=3. Parent index = 1, parent value = 4. 1 < 4, swap -> [3, 1, 5, 4]. idx becomes 1. Parent index = (1-1)//2 = 0, parent value = 3. 1 < 3, swap -> [1, 3, 5, 4]. idx becomes 0, stop. Final heap: [1, 3, 5, 4].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sequential Insertion with Heap Maintenance
ladders.desc: Understand the process of building a heap by inserting elements one at a time and maintaining the heap property.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the naive insertion approach is the iterative process of inserting elements into a heap while ensuring the min-heap property holds after each insertion. This technique builds the heap incrementally: start with an empty heap (or the first element as a trivial heap), and for each subsequent element, add it to the end and then bubble it up as needed. The intuition is that by inserting elements in any order and correcting violations upward, we gradually transform the array into a valid min-heap. This works because the bubble-up operation locally fixes the heap property around the inserted element, and since we process elements sequentially, the heap grows while remaining valid. The key insight is that we treat the array as a binary tree where the index i has children at 2i+1 and 2i+2, and we maintain the invariant that after each insertion, the array up to that point is a min-heap. The algorithm involves a loop over the array indices, calling bubble-up for each index (except the first). This is less efficient than bottom-up heapify but simpler to understand. Time complexity is O(n log n) due to n insertions each taking O(log n). Space is O(1) in-place. This feeds into the full problem as the main control flow. Common mistakes include starting the loop from index 0 (which would try to bubble up the root unnecessarily) or not updating the array in-place. Real code pattern: for i in range(1, len(arr)): bubble_up(arr, i). The step-by-step: 1. Assume arr[0] is the root of a one-element heap. 2. For i from 1 to n-1: a. The element at arr[i] is the new insertion. b. Call bubble_up(arr, i) to place it correctly. 3. After the loop, arr is a min-heap.

examples.trace: Start: arr = [4, 2, 3]. i=1: Insert 2 at index 1, bubble up: parent at index 0 is 4, 2 < 4, swap -> [2, 4, 3]. i=2: Insert 3 at index 2, bubble up: parent at index 0 is 2, 3 >= 2, no swap. Final heap: [2, 4, 3].

Start: arr = [5, 3, 8, 1, 2]. i=1: Insert 3, bubble up: parent 5, 3 < 5, swap -> [3, 5, 8, 1, 2]. i=2: Insert 8, bubble up: parent 3, 8 >= 3, no swap. i=3: Insert 1, bubble up: parent 5, 1 < 5, swap -> [3, 1, 8, 5, 2]; idx=1, parent 3, 1 < 3, swap -> [1, 3, 8, 5, 2]. i=4: Insert 2, bubble up: parent 3, 2 < 3, swap -> [1, 2, 8, 5, 3]; idx=1, parent 1, 2 >= 1, no swap. Final heap: [1, 2, 8, 5, 3].

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Representation of Binary Heap
ladders.desc: Learn how a binary heap is stored in an array and how to navigate parent-child relationships.
ladders.explanation: A binary heap is a complete binary tree that satisfies the heap property (min-heap: parent <= children). It is efficiently stored in an array without pointers, using index calculations to represent the tree structure. The intuition is that by placing the tree elements level by level in the array, we can compute parent and child indices mathematically, saving space and allowing fast access. For a 0-based array: the root is at index 0; for any node at index i, its left child is at 2i+1, right child at 2i+2, and its parent is at floor((i-1)/2), i.e., (i-1)//2 in integer division. This works because in a complete binary tree, the nodes are filled from left to right, so the array indices correspond to a breadth-first traversal. This representation is key for heap operations like bubble-up, which uses parent indices to move up the tree. The building block involves understanding these index relationships and ensuring the array is treated as a heap. Time and space are not directly applicable here, but operations using this representation are O(1) for index calculations. This feeds into higher levels by providing the data structure for the heap. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing formulas incorrectly, assuming the array is sorted (it's not, it's a heap), or not handling cases where children indices may be out of bounds (beyond array length). Real code pattern: parent = (i-1)//2; left_child = 2*i+1; right_child = 2*i+2. The step-by-step: given an array arr, visualize it as a tree: arr[0] is root, arr[1] and arr[2] are its children, etc. To navigate, use the formulas.

examples.trace: Array indices: 0:10, 1:20, 2:30, 3:40, 4:50. For index i=3, parent index = (3-1)//2 = 2//2 = 1. So parent is at index 1, value 20.

examples.output: Left child index 3, right child index 4
examples.trace: Array indices: 0:5, 1:6, 2:7, 3:8, 4:9. For index i=1, left child = 2*1+1 = 3, value 8; right child = 2*1+2 = 4, value 9.

testCases.expected: Left index 5 (out of bounds), right index 6 (out of bounds)
Left index 7 (out of bounds), right index 8 (out of bounds)
Left index 9 (out of bounds), right index 10 (out of bounds)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Swap Elements in Array
ladders.desc: Perform an in-place swap of two elements in an array, a fundamental operation for heap adjustments.
ladders.explanation: Swapping two elements in an array is a basic operation used extensively in heap algorithms, such as during bubble-up when a child and parent need to be exchanged to maintain the heap property. The intuition is simple: to exchange the values at two indices, we use a temporary variable to hold one value while overwriting it, then assign the other value, and finally place the temporary value in the other position. This ensures both values are correctly interchanged without loss of data. The algorithm: given an array arr and indices i and j, if i != j, set temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp. This works in O(1) time and O(1) extra space, making it efficient for in-place modifications. In the context of heaps, swaps occur during bubble-up to move a smaller element upward, and they are critical for maintaining the heap structure. This operation feeds into higher levels as it is used within the bubble-up subroutine. Common mistakes include forgetting to use a temporary variable (which leads to overwriting and loss of data), swapping indices that are out of bounds, or not checking if indices are valid. Real code pattern: def swap(arr, i, j): if i != j: arr[i], arr[j] = arr[j], arr[i]. The step-by-step: for example, swapping arr[0] and arr[1] in [3, 1]: temp = 3, arr[0] = 1, arr[1] = temp, resulting in [1, 3].

examples.trace: Start: arr = [5, 10]. temp = arr[0] = 5. arr[0] = arr[1] = 10. arr[1] = temp = 5. Result: [10, 5].
Start: arr = [2, 4, 6, 8]. temp = arr[1] = 4. arr[1] = arr[3] = 8. arr[3] = temp = 4. Result: [2, 8, 6, 4].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Min-Heap?
ladders.desc: Understand the basic definition and properties of a min-heap data structure.
ladders.explanation: A min-heap is a specialized tree-based data structure that satisfies two key properties: it is a complete binary tree, and it obeys the heap property where the value of each parent node is less than or equal to the values of its children. This means the smallest element is always at the root, making it efficient for access to the minimum element. The intuition is that a min-heap organizes data in a way that prioritizes the smallest items, similar to a priority queue where the highest priority (smallest value) is easily accessible. It is called a 'heap' because it piles elements in a specific order, not to be confused with memory heap. The complete binary tree property ensures the tree is balanced and can be efficiently stored in an array, as covered in higher levels. The heap property ensures that for any node i, arr[i] <= arr[2i+1] and arr[i] <= arr[2i+2] (if those children exist). This foundation is crucial for understanding why bubble-up works: by swapping a smaller child up, we maintain this property. Time complexity for accessing the min is O(1), but insertion and deletion take O(log n). Space is O(n) to store n elements. This concept feeds into all higher levels as the goal of the heapify problem. Common mistakes include confusing min-heap with max-heap (where parent >= children), thinking the array is sorted (it's only partially ordered), or not recognizing the complete binary tree structure. Real code pattern: no code here, but understanding that a min-heap is represented as an array where the properties hold. The step-by-step: to verify a min-heap, check each parent-child pair.

examples.trace: Check root at index 0: value 2. Left child at index 1: value 3, 2 <= 3, ok. Right child at index 2: value 4, 2 <= 4, ok. All parents satisfy the property, and it's a complete tree (indices 0,1,2 are filled). So it is a min-heap.

examples.output: No, it is not a min-heap
examples.trace: Check root at index 0: value 5. Left child at index 1: value 10, 5 <= 10, ok. Right child at index 2: value 3, 5 <= 3? False, violation. So it is not a min-heap.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Bottom-Up Heapify O(n)
approaches.summary: Start from the last non-leaf node and heapify downwards to the root, ensuring each subtree satisfies the heap property. This leverages the fact that most nodes are near the leaves, reducing comparisons.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Build Min-Heap via Bottom-Up Heapify
ladders.desc: Transform an arbitrary array into a valid min-heap using bottom-up heapify in O(n) time.
ladders.explanation: The full problem requires converting an unsorted array into a min-heap, where each parent node is less than or equal to its children. The bottom-up heapify approach achieves this efficiently in O(n) time by starting from the last non-leaf node and working backwards to the root, ensuring each subtree satisfies the heap property. Intuition: Most nodes in a heap are near the leaves, so by processing from bottom to top, we minimize the number of comparisons and swaps needed. The algorithm: 1. Identify the last non-leaf node index as floor((n-2)/2). 2. For i from last non-leaf down to 0: call heapify_down(i). 3. heapify_down(i) ensures the subtree rooted at i is a min-heap by recursively swapping the node with its smallest child if needed. Time: O(n) because the total work is proportional to the sum of heights of all nodes, which sums to O(n). Space: O(1) in-place. Connection to L1: This uses the heapify_down subroutine repeatedly. Common mistakes: Starting from the root (which would be O(n log n)), incorrect index calculations for children, or not handling the base case in heapify_down. Real code pattern: def build_min_heap(arr): n = len(arr); for i in range(n//2 - 1, -1, -1): heapify_down(arr, n, i).

examples.trace: Input: [3,9,2,1,4,5], n=6. Last non-leaf index = 6//2 - 1 = 2. Process i=2 (value 2): children at indices 5 (value 5) and 6 (out of bounds). 2 < 5, no swap needed. Array: [3,9,2,1,4,5]. i=1 (value 9): children at indices 3 (value 1) and 4 (value 4). Smallest child is 1 at index 3. Swap 9 and 1: [3,1,2,9,4,5]. Heapify_down from index 3 (value 9): children at indices 7 and 8 (out of bounds), stop. i=0 (value 3): children at indices 1 (value 1) and 2 (value 2). Smallest child is 1 at index 1. Swap 3 and 1: [1,3,2,9,4,5]. Heapify_down from index 1 (value 3): children at indices 3 (value 9) and 4 (value 4). Compare: 3 < 9 (yes) and 3 < 4 (yes), so 3 is already the smallest. No swap needed. Final: [1,3,2,9,4,5] (valid min-heap).

Input: [7,6,5,4,3,2,1], n=7. Last non-leaf index = 7//2 - 1 = 2. Process i=2 (value 5): children at indices 5 (value 2) and 6 (value 1). Smallest child is 1 at index 6. Swap 5 and 1: [7,6,1,4,3,2,5]. Heapify_down from index 6 (value 5): children at indices 13, 14 (out of bounds), stop. i=1 (value 6): children at indices 3 (value 4) and 4 (value 3). Smallest child is 3 at index 4. Swap 6 and 3: [7,3,1,4,6,2,5]. Heapify_down from index 4 (value 6): children at indices 9, 10 (out of bounds), stop. i=0 (value 7): children at indices 1 (value 3) and 2 (value 1). Smallest child is 1 at index 2. Swap 7 and 1: [1,3,7,4,6,2,5]. Heapify_down from index 2 (value 7): children at indices 5 (value 2) and 6 (value 5). Smallest child is 2 at index 5. Swap 7 and 2: [1,3,2,4,6,7,5]. Heapify_down from index 5 (value 7): children at indices 11, 12 (out of bounds), stop. Final: [1,3,2,4,6,7,5]. Verify: 1≤3,2; 3≤4,6; 2≤7,5 (valid min-heap, though 5 remains at leaf with parent 2 which satisfies 2≤5).

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Heapify Down (Sift Down)
ladders.desc: Ensure a subtree rooted at a given index satisfies the min-heap property by recursively swapping with the smallest child.
ladders.explanation: Heapify down (or sift down) is the core subroutine that fixes a violation at a node by moving it down to its proper position. Intuition: If a node is larger than one of its children in a min-heap, swapping it with the smallest child ensures the parent becomes smaller, but may violate the heap property at the child's position, so we recurse. Algorithm: Given array arr, size n, and index i: 1. Set smallest = i. 2. Compute left child index = 2*i + 1, right child index = 2*i + 2. 3. If left < n and arr[left] < arr[smallest], set smallest = left. 4. If right < n and arr[right] < arr[smallest], set smallest = right. 5. If smallest != i, swap arr[i] and arr[smallest], then recursively call heapify_down(arr, n, smallest). Time: O(log n) in worst case as it traverses the height of the tree. Space: O(log n) for recursion stack, but can be iterative for O(1). Connection to L0: This function is called for each non-leaf node in bottom-up heapify. Common mistakes: Forgetting to recurse after swap, incorrect child index formulas, or not checking array bounds. Real code pattern: def heapify_down(arr, n, i): smallest = i; left = 2*i+1; right = 2*i+2; if left < n and arr[left] < arr[smallest]: smallest = left; if right < n and arr[right] < arr[smallest]: smallest = right; if smallest != i: swap(arr[i], arr[smallest]); heapify_down(arr, n, smallest).

examples.trace: Start: arr=[3,9,2], n=3, i=0. smallest=0. left=1 (value 9), right=2 (value 2). Check left: 9<3? No. Check right: 2<3? Yes, set smallest=2. smallest != 0, swap arr[0] and arr[2]: arr=[2,9,3]. Recursively call heapify_down(arr,3,2). i=2: left=5 out of bounds, right=6 out of bounds, stop. Final: [2,9,3].

Start: arr=[5,3,8,1,4], n=5, i=0. smallest=0. left=1 (3), right=2 (8). Check left: 3<5? Yes, set smallest=1. Check right: 8<3? No. smallest != 0, swap arr[0] and arr[1]: arr=[3,5,8,1,4]. Recursively call heapify_down(arr,5,1). i=1: smallest=1. left=3 (1), right=4 (4). Check left: 1<5? Yes, set smallest=3. Check right: 4<1? No. smallest != 1, swap arr[1] and arr[3]: arr=[3,1,8,5,4]. Recursively call heapify_down(arr,5,3). i=3: left=7 out of bounds, right=8 out of bounds, stop. Final: [3,1,8,5,4].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bottom-Up Processing Order
ladders.desc: Iterate from the last non-leaf node to the root, applying heapify down to build the heap efficiently.
ladders.explanation: The central insight of bottom-up heapify is the processing order: start from the last non-leaf node and move upwards to the root. Intuition: In a heap, nodes near the leaves have small subtrees (height 0 or 1), so heapifying them requires few comparisons. By starting at the bottom, we ensure that when we heapify a higher node, its children are already valid heaps, reducing work. The last non-leaf index is floor((n-2)/2) or n//2 - 1 in 0-based indexing, because the parent of the last element (index n-1) is at floor((n-1-1)/2). Algorithm: 1. Compute last_non_leaf = n//2 - 1. 2. For i from last_non_leaf down to 0: call heapify_down(arr, n, i). Time: O(n) as analyzed by aggregating the work: each node at height h does O(h) work, and sum over all nodes is O(n). Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This order uses heapify_down as a black box. Common mistakes: Starting from 0 to n-1 (top-down, O(n log n)), incorrect last non-leaf index, or skipping the root. Real code pattern: for i in range(n//2 - 1, -1, -1): heapify_down(arr, n, i).

examples.trace: n=6, last_non_leaf = 6//2 - 1 = 2. Iterate i=2,1,0. i=2: heapify_down on index 2 (value 2), no swap. i=1: heapify_down on index 1 (value 9), swap with child 1 at index 3, arr becomes [3,1,2,9,4,5], then heapify_down on index 3 stops. i=0: heapify_down on index 0 (value 3), swap with child 1 at index 1, arr becomes [1,3,2,9,4,5], then heapify_down on index 1 swaps 3 with 4, arr becomes [1,4,2,9,3,5], then heapify_down on index 4 stops. Final adjustments as in L0.

n=7, last_non_leaf = 7//2 - 1 = 2. i=2: heapify_down on index 2 (value 5), swap with child 1 at index 6, arr becomes [7,6,1,4,3,2,5]. i=1: heapify_down on index 1 (value 6), swap with child 3 at index 4, arr becomes [7,3,1,4,6,2,5]. i=0: heapify_down on index 0 (value 7), swap with child 1 at index 2, arr becomes [1,3,7,4,6,2,5], then heapify_down on index 2 swaps 7 with 2, arr becomes [1,3,2,4,6,7,5], then heapify_down on index 5 stops. Final as in L0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Identify Last Non-Leaf Node
ladders.desc: Compute the index of the last node that has at least one child in a heap represented as an array.
ladders.explanation: In a heap stored as an array, leaves are nodes with no children. The last non-leaf node is the parent of the last element, which allows us to start bottom-up heapify from there. Intuition: In a complete binary tree, if the last element is at index n-1, its parent is at floor((n-1-1)/2) = floor((n-2)/2). Since array indices start at 0, the children of index i are at 2i+1 and 2i+2. Thus, the last non-leaf index is the largest i such that 2i+1 < n. Solving: i < (n-1)/2, so i = floor((n-2)/2). For example, n=6: floor((6-2)/2)=2. In code, this simplifies to n//2 - 1 for integer division. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This index determines where to start the bottom-up loop. Common mistakes: Using n/2 without floor, or starting from n-1. Real code pattern: last_non_leaf = n//2 - 1.

examples.trace: n=1, last_non_leaf = 1//2 - 1 = 0 - 1 = -1. Since there are no non-leaf nodes (only one node is a leaf).
n=6, last_non_leaf = 6//2 - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2. Verify: Children of index 2 are at 5 and 6 (0-based), so index 5 is within n=6, but index 6 is not, so it has one child, making it non-leaf.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Swap Array Elements
ladders.desc: Exchange the values at two indices in an array to maintain heap property during heapify.
ladders.explanation: Swapping is a fundamental operation used in heapify to move elements to their correct positions. Intuition: When a parent node violates the min-heap property (i.e., it is larger than a child), we swap it with the smallest child to restore the property. This operation is O(1) and in-place. Algorithm: Given array arr and indices i and j: temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp. In Python, can use arr[i], arr[j] = arr[j], arr[i]. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L1: Swapping is performed within heapify_down when smallest != i. Common mistakes: Swapping without storing a temporary variable (if not using tuple unpacking), or swapping incorrect indices. Real code pattern: def swap(arr, i, j): arr[i], arr[j] = arr[j], arr[i].

examples.trace: Start: arr=[3,9,2]. Swap arr[0] and arr[2]. temp = arr[0] = 3. arr[0] = arr[2] = 2. arr[2] = temp = 3. Result: [2,9,3].
Start: arr=[5,3,8,1,4]. Swap arr[1] and arr[3]. temp = arr[1] = 3. arr[1] = arr[3] = 1. arr[3] = temp = 3. Result: [5,1,8,3,4].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Representation of Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Understand how a complete binary tree is stored in an array using index relationships.
ladders.explanation: A heap is a complete binary tree, which can be efficiently represented as an array without pointers. Intuition: In a complete binary tree, all levels are filled except possibly the last, which is filled from left to right. This allows mapping each node to an array index such that parent-child relationships are computed arithmetically. For 0-based indexing: if a node is at index i, its left child is at 2i+1, right child at 2i+2, and its parent is at floor((i-1)/2). This representation saves space and allows fast access. Example: Array [1,2,3,4,5] represents a tree where root 1 has children 2 (index 1) and 3 (index 2), and 2 has children 4 (index 3) and 5 (index 4). Time to access children or parent: O(1). Space: O(n) for array. Connection to L4: Swapping array elements corresponds to moving nodes in the tree. Common mistakes: Using 1-based indexing formulas incorrectly, or assuming the tree is not complete. Real code pattern: left_child = 2*i+1; right_child = 2*i+2; parent = (i-1)//2.

examples.output: Left child at index 3 (40), right child at index 4 (50)
examples.trace: Given array [10,20,30,40,50]. For index i=1 (value 20): left child index = 2*1+1 = 3, value arr[3]=40; right child index = 2*1+2 = 4, value arr[4]=50.
Given array [5,3,8,1,4]. For index i=3 (value 1): parent index = floor((3-1)/2) = floor(2/2) = 1, value arr[1]=3.
